parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 1–13

passage.text: The Dead Sea Scrolls

In late 1946 or early 1947, three Bedouin teenagers were tending their goats and sheep near the ancient settlement of Qumran, located on the northwest shore of the Dead Sea in what is now known as the West Bank. One of these young shepherds tossed a rock into an opening on the side of a cliff and was surprised to hear a shattering sound. He and his companions later entered the cave and stumbled across a collection of large clay jars, seven of which contained scrolls with writing on them. The teenagers took the seven scrolls to a nearby town where they were sold for a small sum to a local antiquities dealer. Word of the find spread, and Bedouins and archaeologists eventually unearthed tens of thousands of additional scroll fragments from 10 nearby caves; together they make up between 800 and 900 manuscripts. It soon became clear that this was one of the greatest archaeological discoveries ever made.

The origin of the Dead Sea Scrolls, which were written around 2,000 years ago between 150 BCE and 70 CE, is still the subject of scholarly debate even today. According to the prevailing theory, they are the work of a population that inhabited the area until Roman troops destroyed the settlement around 70 CE. The area was known as Judea at that time, and the people are thought to have belonged to a group called the Essenes, a devout Jewish sect.

The majority of the texts on the Dead Sea Scrolls are in Hebrew, with some fragments written in an ancient version of its alphabet thought to have fallen out of use in the fifth century BCE. But there are other languages as well. Some scrolls are in Aramaic, the language spoken by many inhabitants of the region from the sixth century BCE to the siege of Jerusalem in 70 CE. In addition, several texts feature translations of the Hebrew Bible into Greek.

The Dead Sea Scrolls include fragments from every book of the Old Testament of the Bible except for the Book of Esther. The only entire book of the Hebrew Bible preserved among the manuscripts from Qumran is Isaiah; this copy, dated to the first century BCE, is considered the earliest biblical manuscript still in existence. Along with biblical texts, the scrolls include documents about sectarian regulations and religious writings that do not appear in the Old Testament.

The writing on the Dead Sea Scrolls is mostly in black or occasionally red ink, and the scrolls themselves are nearly all made of neither parchment (animal skin) or an early form of paper called ‘papyrus’. The only exception is the scroll numbered 3Q15, which was created out of a combination of copper and tin. Known as the Copper Scroll, this curious document features letters chiselled onto metal – perhaps, as some have theorized, to better withstand the passage of time. One of the most intriguing manuscripts from Qumran, this is a sort of ancient treasure map that lists dozens of gold and silver caches. Using an unconventional vocabulary and odd spelling, it describes 64 underground hiding places that supposedly contain riches buried for safekeeping. of these hoards have been recovered, possibly because the Romans pillaged Judea during the first century CE. According to various hypotheses, the treasure belonged to local people, or was rescued from the Second Temple before its destruction or never existed to begin with.

Some of the Dead Sea Scrolls have been on interesting journeys. In 1948, a Syrian Orthodox archbishop known as Mar Samuel acquired four of the original seven scrolls from a Jerusalem shoemaker and part-time antiquity dealer, paying less than $100 for them. He then travelled to the United States and unsuccessfully offered them to a number of universities, including Yale University. Finally, in 1954, he placed an advertisement in the business newspaper The Wall Street Journal – under the category ‘Miscellaneous Items for Sale’ – that read: ‘Biblical Manuscripts dating back to at least 200 B. C. are for sale. This would be an ideal gift to an educational or religious institution by an individual or group. ’ Fortunately, Israeli archaeologist and statesman Yigael Yadin negotiated their purchase and brought the scrolls back to Jerusalem, where they remain to this day.

In 2017, researchers from the University of Haifa restored and deciphered one of the last untranslated scrolls. The university’s Eshbal Ratson and Jonathan Ben-Dov spent one year reassembling the 60 fragments that make up the scroll. Deciphered from a band of coded text on parchment, the find provides insight into the community of people who wrote it and the 364-day calendar they would have used. The scroll names celebrations that indicate shifts in seasons and details two yearly religious events known from another Dead Sea Scroll. Only one more known scroll remains untranslated.

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 1-5

Complete the notes below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY.
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 1-5Complete the notes below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
The Dead Sea Scrolls

Discovery

Qumran, 1946/1947

  • three Bedouin shepherds in their teens were near an opening on side of cliff
  • heard a noise of breaking when one teenager threw a 1_______
  • teenagers went into the 2_______ and found a number of containers made of 3_______

The scrolls

  • date from between 150 BCE and 70 CE
  • thought to have been written by group of people known as the 4_______
  • written mainly in the 5_______ language
  • most are on religious topics, written using ink on parchment or papyrus
questionGroups.noteContent:
**Discovery**
Qumran, 1946/1947
• three Bedouin shepherds in their teens were near an opening on side of cliff
• heard a noise of breaking when one teenager threw a [1]
• teenagers went into the [2] and found a number of containers made of [3]
**The scrolls**
• date from between 150 BCE and 70 CE
• thought to have been written by group of people known as the [4]
• written mainly in the [5] language
• most are on religious topics, written using ink on parchment or papyrus

questions.prompt: heard a noise of breaking when one teenager threw a 1 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
threw
noise of breaking
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 1: “One of these young shepherds tossed a rock into an opening”
Bước 3: Phân tích
tossed = threw.
=> Đáp án: rock
questions.questionHtml:
The Dead Sea Scrolls

Discovery

Qumran, 1946/1947

  • three Bedouin shepherds in their teens were near an opening on side of cliff
  • heard a noise of breaking when one teenager threw a 1_______
  • teenagers went into the 2_______ and found a number of containers made of 3_______

The scrolls

  • date from between 150 BCE and 70 CE
  • thought to have been written by group of people known as the 4_______
  • written mainly in the 5_______ language
  • most are on religious topics, written using ink on parchment or papyrus

questions.prompt: teenagers went into the 2 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
went into
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 1: “He and his companions later entered the cave”
Bước 3: Phân tích
entered = went into.
=> Đáp án: cave

questions.prompt: found a number of containers made of 3 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
containers made of
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 1: “a collection of large clay jars”
Bước 3: Phân tích
clay jars = bình bằng đất sét.
=> Đáp án: clay

questions.prompt: thought to have been written by group of people known as the 4 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
group of people
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 2: “the people are thought to have belonged to a group called the Essenes”
Bước 3: Phân tích
group called = known as.
=> Đáp án: Essenes

questions.prompt: written mainly in the 5 …………………… language
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
language
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 3: “The majority of the texts ... are in Hebrew”
Bước 3: Phân tích
majority = mainly.
=> Đáp án: Hebrew

questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: The Bedouin teenagers who found the scrolls were disappointed by how little money they received for them.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
disappointed
little money
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

Đoạn 1: “they were sold for a small sum”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Chỉ nói bán với giá thấp.
Không nói các thiếu niên thất vọng.
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: There is agreement among academics about the origin of the Dead Sea Scrolls.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
agreement
origin
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 2: “is still the subject of scholarly debate”
Bước 3: Phân tích
scholarly debate = còn tranh cãi.
Không có sự đồng thuận.
=> Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: Most of the books of the Bible written on the scrolls are incomplete.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
most books
incomplete
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

Đoạn 4: “include fragments from every book” và “The only entire book ... is Isaiah”
Bước 3: Phân tích
fragments = các mảnh rời, không hoàn chỉnh.
Chỉ có Isaiah là nguyên vẹn.
=> Đáp án: TRUE

questions.prompt: The information on the Copper Scroll is written in an unusual way.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
unusual way
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 5: “Using an unconventional vocabulary and odd spelling”
Bước 3: Phân tích
unconventional vocabulary + odd spelling = cách viết bất thường.
=> Đáp án: TRUE

questions.prompt: Mar Samuel was given some of the scrolls as a gift.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
given as a gift
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 6: “acquired four of the original seven scrolls” và “paying less than $100 for them”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Ông mua chứ không được tặng.
=> Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: In the early 1950s, a number of educational establishments in the US were keen to buy scrolls from Mar Samuel.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
educational establishments
keen to buy
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 6: “unsuccessfully offered them to a number of universities”
Bước 3: Phân tích
unsuccessfully = không thành công.
Các trường không muốn mua.
=> Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: The scroll that was pieced together in 2017 contains information about annual occasions in the Qumran area 2,000 years ago.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
2017
annual occasions
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn cuối: “The scroll names celebrations that indicate shifts in seasons and details two yearly religious events”
Bước 3: Phân tích
yearly religious events = các dịp thường niên.
=> Đáp án: TRUE

questions.prompt: Academics at the University of Haifa are currently researching how to decipher the final scroll
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
currently researching
final scroll
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn cuối: “Only one more known scroll remains untranslated.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Chỉ nói còn 1 scroll chưa dịch.
Không nói hiện họ đang nghiên cứu giải mã nó.
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 14–26
parts.passageTitle: A second attempt at domesticating the tomato

passage.text: A second attempt at domesticating the tomato

This approach relies on the revolutionary CRISPR genome editing technique, in which changes are deliberately made to the DNA of a living cell, allowing genetic material to be added, removed or altered. The technique could not only improve existing crops, but could also be used to turn thousands of wild plants into useful and appealing foods. In fact, a third team in the US has already begun to do this with a relative of the tomato called the groundcherry.
This fast-track domestication could help make the world’s food supply healthier and far more resistant to diseases, such as the rust fungus devastating wheat crops.

‘This could transform what we eat,’ says Jorg Kudla at the University of Munster in Germany, a member of the Brazilian team. ‘There are 50,000 edible plants in the world, but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops. ’

‘We can now mimic the known domestication course of major crops like rice, maize, sorghum or others,’ says Caixia Gao of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing. ‘Then we might try to domesticate plants that have never been domesticated. ’

B. Wild tomatoes, which are native to the Andes region in South America, produce pea-sized fruits. Over many generations, peoples such as the Aztecs and Incas transformed the plant by selecting and breeding plants with mutations* in their genetic structure, which resulted in desirable traits such as larger fruit.

But every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost. And sometimes the desirable mutations come with less desirable traits. For instance, the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour.

By comparing the genomes of modern plants to those of their wild relatives, biologists have been working out what genetic changes occurred as plants were domesticated. The teams in Brazil and China have now used this knowledge to reintroduce these changes from scratch while maintaining or even enhancing the desirable traits of wild strains.

C. Kudla’s team made six changes altogether. For instance, they tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT, and increased the number of tomatoes per truss by editing another called MULTIFLORA.

While the historical domestication of tomatoes reduced levels of the red pigment lycopene – thought to have potential health benefits – the team in Brazil managed to boost it instead. The wild tomato has twice as much lycopene as cultivated ones; the newly domesticated one has five times as much.
‘They are quite tasty,’ says Kudla. ‘A little bit strong. And very aromatic. ’

The team in China re-domesticated several strains of wild tomatoes with desirable traits lost in domesticated tomatoes. In this way they managed to create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race, which can devastate yields. They also created another strain that is more salt tolerant – and has higher levels of vitamin C.

D. Meanwhile, Joyce Van Eck at the Boyce Thompson Institute in New York state decided to use the same approach to domesticate the groundcherry or goldenberry (Physalis pruinosa) for the first time. This fruit looks similar to the closely related Cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana).

Groundcherries are already sold to a limited extent in the US but they are hard to produce because the plant has a sprawling growth habit and the small fruits fall off the branches when ripe. Van Eck’s team has edited the plants to increase fruit size, make their growth more compact and to stop fruits dropping. ‘There’s potential for this to be a commercial crop,’ says Van Eck. But she adds that taking the work further would be expensive because of the need to pay for a licence for the CRISPR technology and get regulatory approval.

E. This approach could boost the use of many obscure plants, says Jonathan Jones of the Sainsbury Lab in the UK. But it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers that they become new staple crops, he thinks.

The three teams already have their eye on other plants that could be ‘catapulted into the mainstream’, including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea. By choosing wild plants that are drought or heat tolerant, says Gao, we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms.
But Kudla didn’t want to reveal which species were in his team’s sights, because CRISPR has made the process so easy. ‘Any one with the right skills could go to their lab and do this. ’
* mutations: changes in an organism’s genetic structure that can be passed down to later generations

parts.passageHtml:
A second attempt at domesticating the tomato

A. It took at least 3,000 years for humans to learn how to domesticate the wild tomato and cultivate it for food. Now two separate teams in Brazil and China have done it all over again in less than three years. And they have done it better in some ways, as the re-domesticated tomatoes are more nutritious than the ones we eat at present.

This approach relies on the revolutionary CRISPR genome editing technique, in which changes are deliberately made to the DNA of a living cell, allowing genetic material to be added, removed or altered. The technique could not only improve existing crops, but could also be used to turn thousands of wild plants into useful and appealing foods. In fact, a third team in the US has already begun to do this with a relative of the tomato called the groundcherry.

This fast-track domestication could help make the world’s food supply healthier and far more resistant to diseases, such as the rust fungus devastating wheat crops.

‘This could transform what we eat,’ says Jorg Kudla at the University of Munster in Germany, a member of the Brazilian team. ‘There are 50,000 edible plants in the world, but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops. ’

‘We can now mimic the known domestication course of major crops like rice, maize, sorghum or others,’ says Caixia Gao of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing. ‘Then we might try to domesticate plants that have never been domesticated. ’

B. Wild tomatoes, which are native to the Andes region in South America, produce pea-sized fruits. Over many generations, peoples such as the Aztecs and Incas transformed the plant by selecting and breeding plants with mutations* in their genetic structure, which resulted in desirable traits such as larger fruit.

But every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost. And sometimes the desirable mutations come with less desirable traits. For instance, the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour.

By comparing the genomes of modern plants to those of their wild relatives, biologists have been working out what genetic changes occurred as plants were domesticated. The teams in Brazil and China have now used this knowledge to reintroduce these changes from scratch while maintaining or even enhancing the desirable traits of wild strains.

C. Kudla’s team made six changes altogether. For instance, they tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT, and increased the number of tomatoes per truss by editing another called MULTIFLORA.

While the historical domestication of tomatoes reduced levels of the red pigment lycopene – thought to have potential health benefits – the team in Brazil managed to boost it instead. The wild tomato has twice as much lycopene as cultivated ones; the newly domesticated one has five times as much.

‘They are quite tasty,’ says Kudla. ‘A little bit strong. And very aromatic. ’

The team in China re-domesticated several strains of wild tomatoes with desirable traits lost in domesticated tomatoes. In this way they managed to create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race, which can devastate yields. They also created another strain that is more salt tolerant – and has higher levels of vitamin C.

D. Meanwhile, Joyce Van Eck at the Boyce Thompson Institute in New York state decided to use the same approach to domesticate the groundcherry or goldenberry (Physalis pruinosa) for the first time. This fruit looks similar to the closely related Cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana).

Groundcherries are already sold to a limited extent in the US but they are hard to produce because the plant has a sprawling growth habit and the small fruits fall off the branches when ripe. Van Eck’s team has edited the plants to increase fruit size, make their growth more compact and to stop fruits dropping. ‘There’s potential for this to be a commercial crop,’ says Van Eck. But she adds that taking the work further would be expensive because of the need to pay for a licence for the CRISPR technology and get regulatory approval.

E. This approach could boost the use of many obscure plants, says Jonathan Jones of the Sainsbury Lab in the UK. But it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers that they become new staple crops, he thinks.

The three teams already have their eye on other plants that could be ‘catapulted into the mainstream’, including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea. By choosing wild plants that are drought or heat tolerant, says Gao, we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms.

But Kudla didn’t want to reveal which species were in his team’s sights, because CRISPR has made the process so easy. ‘Any one with the right skills could go to their lab and do this. ’

———————-

* mutations: changes in an organism’s genetic structure that can be passed down to later generations

questionGroups.blockHtml:
List of options

A. Paragraph A

B. Paragraph B

C. Paragraph C

D. Paragraph D

E. Paragraph E

14_______  a reference to a type of tomato that can resist a dangerous infection.

15_______  an explanation of how problems can arise from focusing only on a certain type of tomato plant.

16_______  a number of examples of plants that are not cultivated at present but could be useful as food sources.

17_______  a comparison between the early domestication of the tomato and more recent research

18_______  a personal reaction to the flavour of a tomato that has been genetically edited
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: a reference to a type of tomato that can resist a dangerous infection.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
dangerous infection
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn C: “create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race”
Bước 3: Phân tích
dangerous infection = disease
resistant = có khả năng chống lại
Đoạn này nói về loại cà chua có thể kháng bệnh bacterial spot race.
=> Đáp án: C

questions.prompt: an explanation of how problems can arise from focusing only on a certain type of tomato plant.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
problems
certain type of tomato plant
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn B: “every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đoạn này giải thích việc chỉ chọn một số cây có đặc điểm mong muốn để lai tạo sẽ gây mất đa dạng di truyền và kéo theo đặc điểm không mong muốn.
=> Đây chính là “problems can arise from focusing only on a certain type of tomato plant”.
=> Đáp án: B

questions.prompt: a number of examples of plants that are not cultivated at present but could be useful as food sources.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
plants not cultivated at present
useful as food sources
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn E: “including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đoạn E đưa ra nhiều ví dụ về các loài cây hiện chưa phổ biến nhưng có thể trở thành nguồn thực phẩm hữu ích trong tương lai.
=> Đáp án: E

questions.prompt: a comparison between the early domestication of the tomato and more recent research
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
comparison
early domestication
recent research
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn A: “It took at least 3,000 years … Now two separate teams … in less than three years.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đoạn này so sánh:
quá trình thuần hóa cà chua thời xưa mất 3000 năm
nghiên cứu hiện đại chỉ mất dưới 3 năm
=> Đáp án: A

questions.prompt: a personal reaction to the flavour of a tomato that has been genetically edited
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
personal reaction
flavour
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn C: “‘They are quite tasty,’ says Kudla. ‘A little bit strong. And very aromatic.’”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đây là nhận xét cá nhân về hương vị của cà chua đã chỉnh sửa gen.
=> Đáp án: C

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 19-23

A Jorg Kudla, B Caixia Gao, C Joyce Van Eck, D Jonathan Jones
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 19-23A Jorg Kudla, B Caixia Gao, C Joyce Van Eck, D Jonathan Jones
questionGroups.blockHtml:
List of features

A  Jorg Kudla

B  Caixia Gao

C  Joyce Van Eck

D  Jonathan Jones

19_______ Domestication of certain plants could allow them to adapt to future environmental challenges.

20_______ The idea of growing and eating unusual plants may not be accepted on a large scale.

21_______ It is not advisable for the future direction of certain research to be made public.

22_______ Present efforts to domesticate one wild fruit are limited by the costs involved.

23_______ Humans only make use of a small proportion of the plant food available on Earth.
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: Domestication of certain plants could allow them to adapt to future environmental challenges.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
future environmental challenges
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn E: “we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Người nói là Caixia Gao.
Ý nói cây trồng có thể thích nghi với biến đổi khí hậu trong tương lai.
=> Đáp án: B (Caixia Gao)
questions.questionHtml:
List of features

A  Jorg Kudla

B  Caixia Gao

C  Joyce Van Eck

D  Jonathan Jones

19_______ Domestication of certain plants could allow them to adapt to future environmental challenges.

20_______ The idea of growing and eating unusual plants may not be accepted on a large scale.

21_______ It is not advisable for the future direction of certain research to be made public.

22_______ Present efforts to domesticate one wild fruit are limited by the costs involved.

23_______ Humans only make use of a small proportion of the plant food available on Earth.

questions.prompt: The idea of growing and eating unusual plants may not be accepted on a large scale.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
unusual plants
may not be accepted
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn E: “it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Người nói là Jonathan Jones.
Ông cho rằng thực phẩm mới khó được chấp nhận rộng rãi.
=> Đáp án: D (Jonathan Jones)

questions.prompt: It is not advisable for the future direction of certain research to be made public.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
not advisable
future direction
made public
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn E: “Kudla didn’t want to reveal which species were in his team’s sights”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Jorg Kudla không muốn tiết lộ nghiên cứu tương lai vì công nghệ CRISPR quá dễ áp dụng.
=> Đáp án: A (Jorg Kudla)

questions.prompt: Present efforts to domesticate one wild fruit are limited by the costs involved.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
limited by costs
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn D: “taking the work further would be expensive”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Người nói là Joyce Van Eck.
=> Đáp án: C (Joyce Van Eck)

questions.prompt: Humans only make use of a small proportion of the plant food available on Earth.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
small proportion
plant food available
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn A: “50,000 edible plants … 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Người nói là Jorg Kudla.
Ý nói con người chỉ sử dụng một phần rất nhỏ số thực vật ăn được trên thế giới.
=> Đáp án: A (Jorg Kudla)

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 24–26

Complete the sentences below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 24–26Complete the sentences below.Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questionGroups.blockHtml:
• An undesirable trait such as loss of 24_______ may be caused by a mutation in a tomato gene.

• By modifying one gene in a tomato plant, researchers made the tomato three times its original 25_______

• A type of tomato which was not badly affected by 26_______ , and was rich in vitamin C, was produced by a team of researchers in China.
questionGroups.noteContent:
• An undesirable trait such as loss of [24] may be caused by a mutation in a tomato gene.
• By modifying one gene in a tomato plant, researchers made the tomato three times its original [25]
• A type of tomato which was not badly affected by [26], and was rich in vitamin C, was produced by a team of researchers in China.

questions.prompt: An undesirable trait such as loss of ……………………… may be caused by a mutation in a tomato gene.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
undesirable trait
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn B: “the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
undesirable trait = loss of flavour
=> Đáp án: flavour
questions.questionHtml:
• An undesirable trait such as loss of 24_______ may be caused by a mutation in a tomato gene.

• By modifying one gene in a tomato plant, researchers made the tomato three times its original 25_______

• A type of tomato which was not badly affected by 26_______ , and was rich in vitamin C, was produced by a team of researchers in China.

questions.prompt: By modifying one gene in a tomato plant, researchers made the tomato three times its original ………………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
three times
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn C: “they tripled the size of fruit”
Bước 3: Phân tích
tripled the size = tăng gấp ba kích thước
=> Đáp án: size

questions.prompt: A type of tomato which was not badly affected by ………………………, and was rich in vitamin C, was produced by a team of researchers in China.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
vitamin C
not badly affected
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn C: “another strain that is more salt tolerant – and has higher levels of vitamin C.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
salt tolerant = chịu mặn tốt = không bị ảnh hưởng nhiều bởi muối
=> Đáp án: salt

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 27–40

passage.text: Insight or evolution?
Two scientists consider the origins of discoveries and other innovative behavior

Scientific discovery is popularly believed to result from the sheer genius of such intellectual stars as naturalist Charles Darwin and theoretical physicist Albert Einstein. Our view of such unique contributions to science often disregards the person’s prior experience and the efforts of their lesser-known predecessors. Conventional wisdom also places great weight on insight in promoting breakthrough scientific achievements, as if ideas spontaneously pop into someone’s head – fully formed and functional.

There may be some limited truth to this view. However, we believe that it largely misrepresents the real nature of scientific discovery, as well as that of creativity and innovation in many other realms of human endeavor.

Setting aside such greats as Darwin and Einstein – whose monumental contributions are duly celebrated – we suggest that innovation is more a process of trial and error, where two steps forward may sometimes come with one step back, as well as one or more stops to the right or left. This evolutionary view of human innovation undermines the notion of creative genius and recognizes the cumulative nature of scientific progress.

Consider one unheralded scientist: John Nicholson, a mathematical physicist working in the 1910s who postulated the existence of ‘proto-elements’ in outer space. By combining different numbers of weights of these proto-elements’ atoms, Nicholson could recover the weights of all the elements in the then-known periodic table. These successes are all the more noteworthy given the fact that Nicholson was wrong about the presence of proto-elements: they do not actually exist. Yet, amid his often fanciful theories and wild speculations, Nicholson also proposed a novel theory about the structure of atoms. Niels Bohr, the Nobel prize-winning father of modern atomic theory, jumped off from this interesting idea to conceive his now-famous model of the atom.

What are we to make of this story? One might simply conclude that science is a collective and cumulative enterprise. That may be true, but there may be a deeper insight to be gleaned. We propose that science is constantly evolving, much as species of animals do. In biological systems, organisms may display new characteristics that result from random genetic mutations. In the same way, random, arbitrary or accidental mutations of ideas may help pave the way for advances in science. If mutations prove beneficial, then the animal or the scientific theory will continue to thrive and perhaps reproduce.

Support for this evolutionary view of behavioral innovation comes from many domains. Consider one example of an influential innovation in US horseracing. The so-called ‘acey-deucy’ stirrup placement, in which the rider’s foot in his left stirrup is placed as much as 25 centimeters lower than the right, is believed to confer important speed advantages when turning on oval tracks. It was developed by a relatively unknown jockey named Jackie Westrope. Had Westrope conducted methodical investigations or examined extensive film records in a shrewd plan to outrun his rivals? Had he foreseen the speed advantage that would be conferred by riding acey-deucy? No. He suffered a leg injury, which left him unable to fully bend his left knee. His modification just happened to coincide with enhanced left-hand turning performance. This led to the rapid and widespread adoption of riding acey-deucy by many riders, a racing style which continues in today’s thoroughbred racing.

Plenty of other stories show that fresh advances can arise from error, misadventure, and also pure serendipity – a happy accident. For example, in the early 1970s, two employees of the company 3M each had a problem: Spencer Silver had a product – a glue which was only slightly sticky – and no use for it, while his colleague Art Fry was trying to figure out how to affix temporary bookmarks in his hymn book without damaging its pages. The solution to both these problems was invention of the brilliantly simple yet phenomenally successful Post-it Note. Such examples give lie to the claim that ingenious, designing minds are responsible for human creativity and invention. Far more banal and mechanical forces may be at work; forces that are fundamentally connected to the laws of science.

The notions of insight, creativity and genius are often invoked, but they remain vague and of doubtful scientific utility, especially when one considers the diverse and enduring contributions of individuals such as Plato, Leonardo da Vinci, William Shakespeare, Ludwig van Beethoven, Galileo Galilei, Isaac Newton, Johannes Kepler, Marie Curie, Louis Pasteur and Thomas Edison. These notions merely label rather than explain the evolution of human innovations. We need another approach, and there is a promising candidate.

The Law of Effect was advanced by psychologist Edward Thorndike in 1898, some 40 years after Charles Darwin published his groundbreaking book On the Origin of Species. This simple law holds that organisms tend to repeat successful behaviors and to refrain from performing unsuccessful ones. Just like Darwin’s Law of Natural Selection, the Law of Effect involves an entirely mechanical process of variation and selection, without any end objective in sight.

Of course, the origin of human innovation demands much further study. In particular, the provenance of the raw material on which the Law of Effect operates is not as clearly known as that of the genetic mutations on which the Law of Natural Selection operates. The generation of novel ideas and behaviors may not be entirely random, but constrained by prior successes and failures – of the current individual (such as Bohr) or of predecessors (such as Nicholson).
The time seems right for abandoning the naïve notion of intelligent design and genius, and for scientifically exploring the true origins of creative behavior.

parts.passageHtml:
Insight or evolution?

Two scientists consider the origins of discoveries and other innovative behavior

Scientific discovery is popularly believed to result from the sheer genius of such intellectual stars as naturalist Charles Darwin and theoretical physicist Albert Einstein. Our view of such unique contributions to science often disregards the person’s prior experience and the efforts of their lesser-known predecessors. Conventional wisdom also places great weight on insight in promoting breakthrough scientific achievements, as if ideas spontaneously pop into someone’s head – fully formed and functional.

There may be some limited truth to this view. However, we believe that it largely misrepresents the real nature of scientific discovery, as well as that of creativity and innovation in many other realms of human endeavor.

Setting aside such greats as Darwin and Einstein – whose monumental contributions are duly celebrated – we suggest that innovation is more a process of trial and error, where two steps forward may sometimes come with one step back, as well as one or more stops to the right or left. This evolutionary view of human innovation undermines the notion of creative genius and recognizes the cumulative nature of scientific progress.

Consider one unheralded scientist: John Nicholson, a mathematical physicist working in the 1910s who postulated the existence of ‘proto-elements’ in outer space. By combining different numbers of weights of these proto-elements’ atoms, Nicholson could recover the weights of all the elements in the then-known periodic table. The generation of novel ideas and behaviors may not be entirely random, but constrained by prior successes and failures – of the current individual (such as Bohr) or of predecessors (such as Nicholson).

questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: 27 The purpose of the first paragraph is to
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
first paragraph
purpose

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 1:
“Scientific discovery is popularly believed to result from the sheer genius…”
“Conventional wisdom also places great weight on insight…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đoạn đầu chỉ đưa ra quan niệm phổ biến của mọi người về khám phá khoa học và thiên tài.
Tác giả chưa phản bác ngay mà chỉ giới thiệu niềm tin phổ biến.
=> outline a common assumption = trình bày một giả định phổ biến.
=> Đáp án: D

questions.prompt: 28 What are the writers doing in the second paragraph?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
second paragraph
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 2: “There may be some limited truth to this view. However, we believe that it largely misrepresents the real nature of scientific discovery…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
misrepresents = trình bày sai lệch
Tác giả cho rằng quan điểm ở đoạn 1 không đúng với bản chất thật của sáng tạo khoa học.
=> criticising an opinion
=> Đáp án: A

questions.prompt: 29 In the third paragraph, what do the writers suggest about Darwin and Einstein?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Darwin and Einstein
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 3: “Setting aside such greats as Darwin and Einstein … we suggest that innovation is more a process of trial and error…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
setting aside = xem như ngoại lệ
Tác giả cho rằng Darwin và Einstein là trường hợp đặc biệt, còn đa số đổi mới là quá trình thử-sai.
=> They represent an exception to a general rule.
=> Đáp án: A

options.label: They represent an exception to a general rule.
Their way of working has been misunderstood.
They are an ideal which others should aspire to.

questions.prompt: 30 John Nicholson is an example of a person whose idea
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
John Nicholson
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 4: “Niels Bohr … jumped off from this interesting idea to conceive his now-famous model of the atom.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Ý tưởng của Nicholson giúp Bohr phát triển mô hình nguyên tử nổi tiếng.
=> laid the foundations for someone else’s breakthrough
=> Đáp án: C

options.label: established his reputation as an influential scientist.
was only fully understood at a later point in history.
laid the foundations for someone else’s breakthrough.
initially met with skepticism from the scientific community.

questions.prompt: 31 What is the key point of interest about the ‘acey-deucy’ stirrup placement?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
acey-deucy
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 6: “He suffered a leg injury, which left him unable to fully bend his left knee.”

“His modification just happened to coincide with enhanced left-hand turning performance.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Phát minh này xuất hiện đơn giản vì ông ấy bị chấn thương chân.
=> the simple reason why it was invented
=> Đáp án: A

options.label: the simple reason why it was invented
the enthusiasm with which it was adopted
the research that went into its development
the cleverness of the person who first used it

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 32–36

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 32–36 on your answer sheet, write
YES  if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO  if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN  if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 32–36Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?In boxes 32–36 on your answer sheet, writeYES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writerNO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writerNOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questionGroups.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.prompt: 32 Acknowledging people such as Plato or da Vinci as geniuses will help us understand the process by which great minds create new ideas.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Plato
understand the process
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 8: “These notions merely label rather than explain the evolution of human innovations.”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Tác giả nói việc gọi ai đó là thiên tài chỉ là “gắn nhãn”, không giúp giải thích quá trình sáng tạo.
=> Câu hỏi nói ngược lại.
=> Đáp án: NO

questions.prompt: 33 The Law of Effect was discovered at a time when psychologists were seeking a scientific reason why creativity occurs.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
psychologists
scientific reason
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 9: “The Law of Effect was advanced by psychologist Edward Thorndike in 1898…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Đoạn này không nói các nhà tâm lý học đang tìm lời giải cho sáng tạo.
=> Không có thông tin.
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: 34 The Law of Effect states that no planning is involved in the behaviour of organisms.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
no planning involved
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 9: “without any end objective in sight”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Không có mục tiêu cuối cùng ≠ hoàn toàn không có kế hoạch.
=> Câu hỏi diễn giải quá mức.
=> Đáp án: NO

questions.prompt: 35 The Law of Effect sets out clear explanations about the sources of new ideas and behaviours.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
clear explanations
sources of new ideas
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 10: “the provenance of the raw material … is not as clearly known…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
not as clearly known = chưa được hiểu rõ
=> Câu hỏi nói “clear explanations” là sai.
=> Đáp án: NO

questions.prompt: 36 Many scientists are now turning away from the notion of intelligent design and genius.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
many scientists
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn cuối: “The time seems right for abandoning the naïve notion of intelligent design and genius…”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Tác giả chỉ nêu quan điểm cá nhân.
Không nói “nhiều nhà khoa học” đang làm vậy.
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questionGroups.groupHtml:
Questions 37-40

A invention B goals C compromise D mistakes E luck F inspiration G experiments
questionGroups.instruction: Questions 37-40A invention B goals C compromise D mistakes E luck F inspiration G experiments
questionGroups.blockHtml:
The origins of creative behaviour

The traditional view of scientific discovery is that breakthroughs happen when a single great mind has sudden 37_______. Although this can occur, it is not often the case. Advances are more likely to be the result of a longer process. In some cases, this process involves 38_______, such as Nicholson’s theory about proto-elements. In others, simple necessity may provoke innovation, as with Westrope’s decision to modify the position of his riding stirrups. There is also often an element of 39_______, for example, the coincidence of ideas that led to the invention of the Post-It note. With both the Law of Natural Selection and the Law of Effect, there may be no clear 40_______ involved, but merely a process of variation and selection.
questionGroups.noteContent: The traditional view of scientific discovery is that breakthroughs happen when a single great mind has sudden [37]. Although this can occur, it is not often the case. Advances are more likely to be the result of a longer process. In some cases, this process involves [38], such as Nicholson’s theory about proto-elements. In others, simple necessity may provoke innovation, as with Westrope’s decision to modify the position of his riding stirrups. There is also often an element of [39], for example, the coincidence of ideas that led to the invention of the Post-It note. With both the Law of Natural Selection and the Law of Effect, there may be no clear [40] involved, but merely a process of variation and selection.

questions.prompt: breakthroughs happen when a single great mind has sudden 37 …………………… .
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
sudden
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 1: “ideas spontaneously pop into someone’s head”
Bước 3: Phân tích
Ý tưởng xuất hiện bất ngờ = inspiration.
=> Đáp án: F (inspiration)
questions.questionHtml:
The origins of creative behaviour

The traditional view of scientific discovery is that breakthroughs happen when a single great mind has sudden 37_______. Although this can occur, it is not often the case. Advances are more likely to be the result of a longer process. In some cases, this process involves 38_______, such as Nicholson’s theory about proto-elements. In others, simple necessity may provoke innovation, as with Westrope’s decision to modify the position of his riding stirrups. There is also often an element of 39_______, for example, the coincidence of ideas that led to the invention of the Post-It note. With both the Law of Natural Selection and the Law of Effect, there may be no clear 40_______ involved, but merely a process of variation and selection.

questions.prompt: In some cases, this process involves 38 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Nicholson
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 4: “Nicholson was wrong about the presence of proto-elements”
Bước 3: Phân tích
wrong = sai lầm
=> mistakes
=> Đáp án: D (mistakes)

questions.prompt: There is also often an element of 39 ……………………
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Post-It note
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 7: “pure serendipity – a happy accident”
Bước 3: Phân tích
happy accident = sự may mắn/tình cờ
=> luck
=> Đáp án: E (luck)

questions.prompt: there may be no clear 40 …………………… involved
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
no clear
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Đoạn 9: “without any end objective in sight”
Bước 3: Phân tích
end objective = mục tiêu cuối cùng
=> goals
=> Đáp án: B (goals)
